title: Memphis · The Wave That Broke
subtitle: FBI UCR 1985–2019 + MPD NIBRS 2020–2026 · 9 police precincts · real locations

hook.line: Memphis crime, down a third from the 2023 peak
hook.sub: MPD reports · a property-crime wave rose and broke — up close

punchline.sub: −32% in two years, same MPD measure · the wave was property crime — 96% of the fall

copy.seamExplain: The ruler changes here — not the city. Through 2019 the steel-blue bars are the FBI's classic 'index' crimes (dark: property, light: violent). From 2020 each bar splits into MPD's own NIBRS Group A categories — a broader modern count (though MPD's source omits sex crimes), so the level steps up. Compare the shape within one era, never across the dashed line.
copy.chapter2Kicker: CHAPTER 2 · THE LAST 5 YEARS · MPD NIBRS
copy.chapter2Caption: Monthly reports, 2021–2026. Every dot is a real reported incident location, block-level, sampled.
copy.transitionDesc: The chart counted per year. From here the map counts per month across 9 police precincts — every dot a real reported location, published by MPD at block-level precision.
copy.methodRecentSub: the last five years · month by month · real locations
copy.methodDotsSub: Every dot is a real reported incident location, published by MPD at ~3-decimal (block-level) precision. 98.8% map to one of 9 police precincts — the rest counted, disclosed, never invented.
copy.methodFootnote: FBI UCR (through 2019) and MPD NIBRS Group A (2020+) are different measures — shown as two chapters, not one line. MPD's source omits sex crimes and juvenile-specific types.
copy.quizQuestion: Which Memphis police precinct reports the fewest Group A crimes?
copy.sourceLine: Data: Memphis Police Department via City of Memphis Open Data Hub · real block-level locations
copy.coverageText: 98.8% of records mapped to a police precinct
copy.creditsSources: Sources: Memphis Police Department Public Safety Incidents (2020–present) via the City of Memphis Open Data Hub & FBI UCR (1985–2019) via the FBI Crime Data Explorer (ORI TNMPD0000). Precincts: MPD official station areas (9 polygons). MPD's dataset omits sex crimes and juvenile-specific types (an MPD publishing decision) and publishes coordinates at block-level (~3-decimal) precision. FBI UCR index crimes and MPD's NIBRS Group A records are different measures, shown as two chapters and never equated. Basemap: © OpenStreetMap contributors (ODbL).

historyNotes.text: 1985: the record begins — 60,673 index crimes under the FBI measure.
1996: the FBI-era peak — 70,901 index crimes reported.
2019: 53,104 — down 25% from the 1996 peak; the last year under the FBI measure.
2023: the MPD-era peak — 106,788 reports, 71% of them property crimes: the wave's crest.
2025: 72,843 — the era's low. Property fell 43% from the crest; crimes against persons stayed in their narrow range.

annotations.text: Every dot is a real MPD-reported incident location — published at block level (~3-decimal coordinates), never invented.
The wave is property crime: 5,996 property reports this month, while crimes against persons hold near 2,300.
May 2023 — the busiest Group A month of the last five years: 9,932 reports, 7,067 of them property crimes.
Appling Farms — the busiest precinct of the last five years, and the steepest fall: down 36% since 2022.
All nine precincts reported less in 2025 than in 2022 — Group A fell 95,112 → 72,843, −23%.
